ADMIT_ENJOY_VB.title: Verb + Gerund (enjoy swimming, not enjoy to swim)
ADMIT_ENJOY_VB.explanation: Some verbs (enjoy, admit, avoid, finish, etc.) must be followed by gerund (-ing form), not infinitive (to + verb).
examples: ❌ She admitted to make a mistake

AFRAID_FROM_OF.title: Afraid OF (not afraid FROM)
AFRAID_FROM_OF.explanation: The correct preposition with 'afraid' is OF, not FROM. Many adjectives require specific prepositions.

GOOD_IN_AT.title: Good AT (not good IN)
GOOD_IN_AT.explanation: Use 'good AT' for skills and activities, not 'good IN'. We also say 'bad AT', 'terrible AT', 'excellent AT'.

DEPEND_ON.title: Depend ON (not depend OF)
DEPEND_ON.explanation: The correct preposition with 'depend' is ON. 'It depends on the weather' = the weather determines the result.

DOUBLE_NEGATIVE.title: Double Negatives (don't say 'don't have nothing')
DOUBLE_NEGATIVE.explanation: In standard English, don't use two negatives together. Use 'anything' with 'don't', not 'nothing'.

BETWEEN_YOU_AND_I.title: Between you and ME (not between you and I)
BETWEEN_YOU_AND_I.explanation: After prepositions (between, with, for, to), use object pronouns: me, him, her, us, them (NOT I, he, she, we, they).
BETWEEN_YOU_AND_I.cambridge_unit: Unit 82 (I and me, he and him, etc.)

CONFUSION_OF_ME_I.title: Me vs I (subject vs object)
CONFUSION_OF_ME_I.explanation: Use I (subject): I go, John and I went. Use me (object): with me, John and me (in casual speech after 'and').

CONDITIONAL_CLAUSE.title: Conditionals (If I go... / If I went...)
CONDITIONAL_CLAUSE.explanation: First conditional (real future): If + present, will + verb. Second conditional (unreal present): If + past, would + verb. Don't use 'will' or 'would' in the IF clause.
examples: ✅ If I see him, I will tell him (1st conditional)
✅ If I saw him, I would tell him (2nd conditional)
❌ If I will see him, I will tell him
❌ If I would see him, I would tell him

MANY_NN_U.title: Many + Countable (not many + uncountable)
MANY_NN_U.explanation: Use 'many' with countable nouns (books, people). Use 'much' with uncountable nouns (water, money).
examples: ✅ A lot of books / A lot of water (both)

FEWER_LESS.title: Fewer vs Less (countable vs uncountable)
FEWER_LESS.explanation: Use 'fewer' with countable nouns. Use 'less' with uncountable nouns.

MUST_HAVE_TO.title: Must vs Have to
MUST_HAVE_TO.explanation: Must = strong obligation from speaker. Have to = external obligation. Mustn't = prohibited. Don't have to = not necessary.
examples: ✅ I have to work tomorrow (external rule)
❌ You mustn't come (wrong: mustn't ≠ don't have to)

AGREEMENT_SENT_START.title: Subject-Verb Agreement at sentence start
AGREEMENT_SENT_START.explanation: Make sure the verb agrees with the subject, especially at the beginning of sentences.

BEEN_PART_AGREEMENT.title: Been + Past Participle
BEEN_PART_AGREEMENT.explanation: After 'been' (have/has/had been), use the past participle (3rd form), not base form.

IT_IS.title: It's vs Its (contraction vs possessive)
IT_IS.explanation: It's = it is OR it has (contraction with apostrophe). Its = possessive (no apostrophe), like his/her.
examples: ✅ It's been a long day (it has)

I_AM_VB.title: I am + adjective/noun (not I am + base verb)
I_AM_VB.explanation: After 'I am', use an adjective, noun, or present participle (-ing), not base verb.

EN_A_VS_AN.title: Articles: a vs an
EN_A_VS_AN.explanation: Use 'an' before vowel sounds (a, e, i, o, u), 'a' before consonant sounds.
examples: ✅ a book, a university (starts with 'yu' sound)

HAVE_PART_AGREEMENT.title: Grammar Rule: Have Part Agreement
HAVE_PART_AGREEMENT.explanation: This construction doesn't follow standard English grammar patterns.

HE_VERB_AGR.title: Subject-Verb Agreement: He/She/It + verb-s
HE_VERB_AGR.explanation: Add -s/-es to verbs in Present Simple with he, she, it.
examples: ❌ She don't like apples → She doesn't like apples

MD_BASEFORM.title: Grammar Rule: Md Baseform
MD_BASEFORM.explanation: This construction doesn't follow standard English grammar patterns.

MISSING_TO_BEFORE_A_VERB.title: Missing 'to' before infinitive
MISSING_TO_BEFORE_A_VERB.explanation: Many verbs require 'to' before the infinitive form.

MORFOLOGIK_RULE_EN_US.title: Grammar Rule: Morfologik Rule En Us
MORFOLOGIK_RULE_EN_US.explanation: This construction doesn't follow standard English grammar patterns.

MOST_COMPARATIVE.title: Grammar Rule: Most Comparative
MOST_COMPARATIVE.explanation: This construction doesn't follow standard English grammar patterns.

MOST_SUPERLATIVE.title: Grammar Rule: Most Superlative
MOST_SUPERLATIVE.explanation: This construction doesn't follow standard English grammar patterns.

MUCH_COUNTABLE.title: Much vs Many (Countable/Uncountable)
MUCH_COUNTABLE.explanation: Use 'many' with countable nouns, 'much' with uncountable nouns.

NON3PRS_VERB.title: Subject-Verb Agreement: I/You/We/They + base form
NON3PRS_VERB.explanation: Use the base form of the verb (without -s) with I, you, we, and they.

PERS_PRONOUN_AGREEMENT.title: Grammar Rule: Pers Pronoun Agreement
PERS_PRONOUN_AGREEMENT.explanation: This construction doesn't follow standard English grammar patterns.

DID_BASEFORM.title: Past Simple: did + base form
DID_BASEFORM.explanation: After 'did' (questions/negatives), use the base form, not past tense.

THE.title: Articles: the (definite article)
THE.explanation: Use 'the' when both speaker and listener know which thing/person.
examples: ✅ Close the door (we both know which door)
✅ I went to the bank (my bank)

PREP.title: Prepositions
PREP.explanation: Different verbs and adjectives require specific prepositions.

MODAL_VERB_BASEFORM.title: Modal Verbs: must/should/can + base form
MODAL_VERB_BASEFORM.explanation: After modal verbs (can, should, must, etc.), use base form without 'to'.

WORD_ORDER.title: Word Order: Subject + Verb + Object
WORD_ORDER.explanation: English follows SVO order. Adverbs usually don't go between verb and object.

PLURAL_NOUN.title: Plural Nouns
PLURAL_NOUN.explanation: Add -s/-es to make nouns plural. Some nouns are irregular (child→children).

TO_BASEFORM.title: To + infinitive vs -ing
TO_BASEFORM.explanation: Some verbs take 'to + infinitive', others take '-ing'. Must memorize.

BASE_FORM.title: Grammar Rule: Base Form
BASE_FORM.explanation: This construction doesn't follow standard English grammar patterns.

PAST_PARTICIPLE.title: Past Participle (have gone, not have went)
PAST_PARTICIPLE.explanation: After have/has/had, use the past participle (3rd form), not past simple (2nd form).

HAVE_PAST_PARTICIPLE.title: Present Perfect: have/has + past participle
HAVE_PAST_PARTICIPLE.explanation: Use 'have/has + past participle' for actions connected to now.
